text: Cordwood construction (also called stackwall) uses short logs laid widthwise in a wall, set in mortar like bricks. Use logs 12-24 inches long (the wall thickness), debarked and dried for at least 1 year. The mortar mix: 9 parts sand, 3 parts soaked sawdust, 3 parts Portland cement, 2 parts hydrated lime. Apply mortar to the inner and outer edges of each log, leaving a dead air space in the middle for insulation. Fill the center cavity with sawdust insulation.

A post-and-beam foundation uses treated wood or concrete posts set in holes rather than a continuous perimeter foundation. Dig holes below the frost line (check local codes — typically 36-48 inches in northern climates). Set 6x6 treated posts or sonotube forms filled with concrete. Space posts 6-8 feet apart. This approach costs 40-60% less than a poured concrete foundation and requires no heavy equipment.

Earth-bag building uses polypropylene bags filled with earth (subsoil with 30% clay content is ideal) stacked to form walls. Each course is tamped firm and topped with 2 strands of 4-point barbed wire between courses for tensile strength. Walls are typically 15 inches thick, providing excellent thermal mass. This technique is suitable for domes, straight walls, and retaining walls. Cost is extremely low when using on-site soil. Finish with 2-3 coats of earthen or lime plaster for weather protection.

Metal roofing is the preferred choice for off-grid buildings: it lasts 40-70 years, is lightweight, sheds snow easily, and is ideal for rainwater harvesting. Use 26-gauge or heavier steel panels with factory-applied paint finish. Install over purlins (horizontal boards) spaced 24 inches on center. Use rubber-washer screws designed for metal roofing. Ensure a minimum roof pitch of 3:12 (3 inches of rise per 12 inches of run). Overlap panels by at least one corrugation and seal with butyl tape.

Passive solar design uses the building's orientation and materials to heat and cool without mechanical systems. Orient the building's long axis east-west with most windows facing south. In the Northern Hemisphere, south-facing windows receive 2-3 times more solar energy in winter than in summer. Use thermal mass (concrete, stone, or water containers) inside to absorb daytime heat and release it at night. Roof overhangs sized to block summer sun (when the sun is high) while admitting winter sun (when the sun is low).
